name: Run Quick Tasks Without Playbooks
description: Execute one-off commands across multiple hosts using ansible ad-hoc commands for quick checks, updates, and troubleshooting.

steps.title: Ping all hosts
steps.description: Verifies SSH connectivity and Python availability on all managed hosts.

steps.title: Run a shell command
steps.description: Checks disk usage on the root filesystem across all webservers.
steps.tip: Use the 'shell' module for commands with pipes or redirects. Use 'command' for simple commands.

steps.title: Copy a file to all hosts
steps.description: Distributes a file to all hosts with root privileges.

steps.title: Install a package
steps.description: Installs htop on all webservers using the apt module.

steps.title: Gather system facts
steps.description: Retrieves distribution-related facts (OS name, version, release) from a single host.

fullScript:
# Ansible ad-hoc commands
ansible all -m ping
ansible webservers -m shell -a "df -h / | tail -1"
ansible all -m copy -a "src=./motd.txt dest=/etc/motd" --become
ansible webservers -m apt -a "name=htop state=present update_cache=true" --become
ansible web01 -m setup -a "filter=ansible_distribution*"

faq.question: When should I use ad-hoc commands vs playbooks?
faq.answer: Ad-hoc commands are ideal for one-off tasks like checking uptime, restarting a service, or quick file copies. Use playbooks for repeatable, multi-step workflows that should be version-controlled.

faq.question: How do I run an ad-hoc command with sudo?
faq.answer: Add the --become flag (or -b). To specify a different sudo user, add --become-user=username. For a sudo password prompt, add --ask-become-pass.

faq.question: Can I limit ad-hoc commands to specific hosts?
faq.answer: Yes. Use --limit 'host1,host2' or patterns like --limit 'web*' to target specific hosts. You can also use -l as a shorthand.
